---
title: "Chapter 38 — 🧭 Search & Problem Solving"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 37 · 🚨 Anomaly Detection](ch37.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [39 · 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning →](ch39.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Long before neural networks, the dominant view of intelligence was **search**: pose a problem as a space of possible situations and look for a path from where you are to where you want to be. This chapter is about that idea — how to turn a problem into a state space, and how to explore that space cleverly enough to find a solution without examining all of it. Search sits at the root of classical (symbolic) AI and still quietly powers planners, theorem provers, route finders, and game engines today.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Classical & symbolic AI · used to find a sequence of actions from a start state to a goal · the one key idea — *intelligence as systematic exploration of a state space, made tractable by good heuristics*
:::

## 38.1 — Formulating a problem as a state space

The first act of problem solving is not solving — it is **formulation**. You decide what counts as a "situation" and what counts as a "move." Get this right and a hard-looking puzzle becomes a clean graph; get it wrong and you drown in irrelevant detail.

A search problem is specified by five parts:

- **State**: a snapshot of the world relevant to the problem. For a sliding-8-puzzle, the state is the arrangement of tiles; for navigation, it is the city you are in.
- **Initial state**: where you start.
- **Actions** (or successor function): given a state, which moves are legal and what state does each lead to. This implicitly defines the edges of a graph.
- **Goal test**: a predicate that says whether a state is a solution (e.g. "tiles in order," or "in Bucharest").
- **Path cost**: a number we attach to a sequence of actions, usually a sum of per-step **step costs** $c(s, a, s')$. We typically want the cheapest path, not just any path.

The states and actions together form the **state space**: a directed graph where nodes are states and edges are actions. Crucially, this graph is almost never written out in full — it is *generated on demand* by applying the successor function. The 8-puzzle has 181,440 reachable states; a Rubik's cube has about $4.3 \times 10^{19}$. We explore a tiny slice.

A subtle but vital distinction is between a **state** (a configuration of the world) and a **node** (a bookkeeping object in the search). A node points to a state, but it also carries its parent, the action that produced it, and its path cost. Many different nodes can refer to the very same state if we reach that state by different paths — which is exactly why the search machinery needs nodes and not just states.

Here is the same idea as a tiny graph: a few states, the actions that connect them, and a goal we are trying to reach.

<svg viewBox="0 0 360 130" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11">
  <circle cx="40" cy="65" r="16" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <text x="40" y="69" text-anchor="middle">S</text>
  <circle cx="150" cy="30" r="16" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <text x="150" y="34" text-anchor="middle">a</text>
  <circle cx="150" cy="100" r="16" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <text x="150" y="104" text-anchor="middle">b</text>
  <circle cx="260" cy="65" r="16" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <text x="260" y="69" text-anchor="middle">c</text>
  <circle cx="330" cy="65" r="16" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <text x="330" y="69" text-anchor="middle">G</text>
  <line x1="55" y1="58" x2="135" y2="36" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <line x1="55" y1="72" x2="135" y2="94" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <line x1="166" y1="34" x2="245" y2="60" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <line x1="166" y1="96" x2="245" y2="70" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <line x1="276" y1="65" x2="314" y2="65" stroke="currentColor"/>
</svg>

::: {.callout-tip}
Good formulation is abstraction. Strip the state down to exactly what affects the goal and the legal moves — no more. A smaller state space is an exponentially smaller search.
:::

## 38.2 — The generic search algorithm and the frontier

Every search method in this chapter is a variation on one loop. You keep a **frontier** (also called the *open list*) — the set of nodes you have generated but not yet expanded. To **expand** a node means to generate all its successors. You repeatedly pull a node from the frontier, test it for the goal, and push its successors back. What changes between algorithms is *only the order in which you pull from the frontier*.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize frontier with start node] --> B{Frontier empty?}
    B -- yes --> F[Return failure]
    B -- no --> C[Remove a node by strategy]
    C --> D{Goal?}
    D -- yes --> E[Return path]
    D -- no --> G[Expand: add successors to frontier]
    G --> H[Mark state visited]
    H --> B
```

```python
from collections import deque

def search(start, successors, is_goal, take):
    frontier = [(start, [start])]      # node = (state, path)
    seen = {start}                     # graph search: never revisit a state
    while frontier:
        state, path = take(frontier)   # the ONLY thing that varies
        if is_goal(state):
            return path
        for nxt in successors(state):
            if nxt not in seen:
                seen.add(nxt)
                frontier.append((nxt, path + [nxt]))
    return None
# take = pop(0) -> BFS (queue); take = pop() -> DFS (stack)
```

The `seen` set is what makes this **graph search** rather than **tree search**: without it, you would re-expand the same states endlessly along cyclic paths. The cost is memory — you must remember every state visited. Tree search keeps no such record, so it uses less memory but can loop forever in a cyclic space; graph search trades that memory for a guarantee of termination.

Four properties let us compare strategies:

- **Completeness**: if a solution exists, is the algorithm guaranteed to find one?
- **Optimality**: does it find the *least-cost* solution?
- **Time complexity**: how many nodes get generated?
- **Space complexity**: how many nodes must be held in memory at once?

These are usually expressed with **branching factor** $b$ (successors per node) and **depth** $d$ (of the shallowest goal), with $m$ the maximum depth of the space. Keep these four properties in mind: the rest of the chapter is essentially a tour of how different pull-orders score on them.

## 38.3 — Uninformed search: exploring blind

**Uninformed** (or *blind*) search uses no information about a state beyond whether it is the goal. It cannot tell a promising state from a hopeless one — it only follows a fixed order. Four classics cover almost every case.

**Breadth-first search (BFS)** uses a FIFO queue, so it expands all nodes at depth 1 before any at depth 2, and so on. It fans out level by level, like a ripple spreading on water. It is complete and, when every step costs the same, optimal (the shallowest goal is the cheapest). Its curse is memory: it must store the entire frontier, which grows as $O(b^d)$.

**Depth-first search (DFS)** uses a LIFO stack, plunging down one branch before backtracking. Its great virtue is space — it stores only the current path plus the unexplored siblings along it, $O(bm)$. But it is not optimal, and in an infinite or cyclic space (tree-search form) it may never terminate.

**Uniform-cost search (UCS)** is BFS's cost-aware cousin: instead of fewest *edges*, it always expands the node with the lowest *path cost* $g(n)$, using a priority queue. This is exactly Dijkstra's algorithm. It is complete and optimal for any non-negative step costs, which makes it the right choice when edges have different weights and BFS's "fewest edges" no longer means "cheapest."

**Iterative deepening (IDS)** is the clever compromise. Run a depth-limited DFS with limit 0, then 1, then 2, and so on until a goal is found. It marries DFS's $O(bd)$ memory with BFS's completeness and shallowest-goal optimality. The apparent waste — re-searching shallow levels every round — is negligible, because the *last* level dominates the node count: in a tree with branching factor $b$, the bottom level has more nodes than all the levels above it combined, so total work is still $O(b^d)$, only a constant factor more than BFS.

| Strategy | Complete? | Optimal? | Time | Space |
|----------|-----------|----------|------|-------|
| BFS | Yes | Yes (unit cost) | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^d)$ |
| DFS (tree) | No | No | $O(b^m)$ | $O(bm)$ |
| Uniform-cost | Yes | Yes | $O(b^{1+\lfloor C^*/\epsilon\rfloor})$ | same |
| Iterative deepening | Yes | Yes (unit cost) | $O(b^d)$ | $O(bd)$ |

Here $C^*$ is the optimal cost and $\epsilon$ the smallest step cost. Iterative deepening is the default uninformed choice precisely because it gives BFS's guarantees at DFS's memory budget.

::: {.callout-warning}
"Depth-first is faster than breadth-first" is a myth. DFS can be catastrophically slow if it dives down a wrong, deep branch. Its only reliable advantage is memory, not speed.
:::

## 38.4 — Informed search: heuristics and A*

Blind search treats all frontier nodes as equals. **Informed** (or *heuristic*) search adds a **heuristic function** $h(n)$ — a cheap estimate of the cost from state $n$ to the nearest goal. It is the search equivalent of intuition: a hint about which direction is promising, computed without actually solving the rest of the problem. For map navigation, straight-line distance is a natural $h$; for the 8-puzzle, the number of misplaced tiles or the sum of Manhattan distances.

**Greedy best-first search** expands the node with the smallest $h(n)$ — it always lunges toward what looks closest. It is often fast but easily fooled: it ignores the cost already paid, so it can charge confidently down an expensive path and is neither optimal nor complete.

**A\*** fixes this by ranking nodes on the sum

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

where $g(n)$ is the actual cost from the start to $n$ and $h(n)$ is the estimated cost from $n$ to the goal. So $f(n)$ estimates the cost of the *whole path through $n$*. A\* always expands the node with the lowest $f$ — balancing "how far I've come" against "how far I have to go." It is the workhorse of heuristic search: route planners, game pathfinding, and many planners run on it.

```python
import heapq

def astar(start, successors, is_goal, h):
    # frontier ordered by f = g + h
    frontier = [(h(start), 0, start, [start])]   # (f, g, state, path)
    best_g = {start: 0}
    while frontier:
        f, g, state, path = heapq.heappop(frontier)
        if is_goal(state):
            return path
        for nxt, step in successors(state):      # step = edge cost
            ng = g + step
            if nxt not in best_g or ng < best_g[nxt]:
                best_g[nxt] = ng
                heapq.heappush(frontier, (ng + h(nxt), ng, nxt, path + [nxt]))
    return None
```

The two heuristic searches sit at opposite ends, and A\* is the middle ground: greedy uses $h$ alone, uniform-cost uses $g$ alone, and A\* uses their sum. If $h \equiv 0$, every node has $f = g$ and A\* degenerates exactly into uniform-cost search — A\* is UCS *guided* by a heuristic. If instead $g$ were ignored, you would be back to greedy search. That single sum is what buys both speed and the optimality guarantee of the next section.

<svg viewBox="0 0 360 150" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11">
  <circle cx="40" cy="75" r="16" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <text x="40" y="79" text-anchor="middle">S</text>
  <circle cx="180" cy="40" r="16" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <text x="180" y="44" text-anchor="middle">n</text>
  <circle cx="320" cy="75" r="16" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <text x="320" y="79" text-anchor="middle">G</text>
  <line x1="55" y1="68" x2="166" y2="46" stroke="currentColor"/>
  <line x1="194" y1="46" x2="306" y2="68" stroke="currentColor" stroke-dasharray="4 3"/>
  <text x="105" y="44" text-anchor="middle">g(n) actual</text>
  <text x="258" y="44" text-anchor="middle">h(n) estimate</text>
  <text x="180" y="120" text-anchor="middle">f(n) = g(n) + h(n)</text>
</svg>

## 38.5 — Why A* works: admissible and consistent heuristics

A\* is optimal, but only if the heuristic plays fair. Two properties pin this down.

A heuristic is **admissible** if it never *overestimates* the true cost to the goal: $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ for all $n$, where $h^*$ is the real remaining cost. Admissible heuristics are *optimistic* — they always think the goal is at least as close as it really is. The intuition for why this guarantees optimality: A\* will not commit to a goal node until its $f$ is the smallest on the frontier; because $h$ never overestimates, any cheaper solution still lurking on the frontier has an $f$ that is a true lower bound on its real cost, so A\* cannot have walked past it.

A stronger property is **consistency** (or *monotonicity*): for every edge from $n$ to a successor $n'$ with cost $c$,

$$h(n) \le c(n, a, n') + h(n').$$

This is a triangle inequality — the estimate from $n$ can't beat the cost of going to $n'$ and estimating from there. Consistency implies admissibility, and it gives a bonus: $f$ never decreases along any path, so the first time A\* pops a state it has already found that state's optimal $g$. With a consistent heuristic, A\* never needs to reopen a closed state — the graph-search version is provably optimal without the extra bookkeeping of re-expanding nodes.

A\* is not just optimal; it is **optimally efficient**: no algorithm using the same heuristic information can be guaranteed to expand fewer nodes, because any node A\* expands has $f(n) < C^*$ and must be examined by *any* optimal algorithm using that same $h$. The price is memory — like BFS, A\* keeps the whole frontier, $O(b^d)$ in the worst case. Variants such as **IDA\*** (iterative-deepening A\*) trade time for memory by iterating on an $f$-cost threshold instead of storing the frontier, much as IDS did for BFS.

A practical note on building heuristics: a reliable recipe is to solve a **relaxed problem** — one with some constraints removed — and use its exact cost as $h$. Manhattan distance for the 8-puzzle is exactly this: it is the true cost if tiles could pass through one another. Because a relaxed problem is never harder than the real one, its exact cost can never overestimate, so relaxed-problem heuristics are admissible by construction. When you have two admissible heuristics, taking their maximum at each node gives another admissible heuristic that dominates both — a free upgrade.

::: {.callout-warning}
A heuristic that occasionally overestimates can make A\* return a suboptimal path while looking like it's working — it quietly closes the goal too early. If optimality matters, prove your $h$ is admissible; don't assume a "reasonable-looking" estimate is safe.
:::

## 38.6 — Local search: when the path doesn't matter

Everything so far cares about the *path* to the goal. But for many optimization problems — scheduling, circuit layout, the $n$-queens puzzle — we only want a good final state; how we got there is irrelevant. **Local search** exploits this: it keeps a single **current state** (or a few) and tries to improve it by moving to neighbors, using almost no memory. Picture standing on a landscape where altitude is the objective you want to maximize, and groping uphill in the fog.

**Hill climbing** is the simplest version: always step to the best neighbor; stop when no neighbor is better. It is fast and memory-light but gets stuck. The classic failures form a vocabulary worth knowing:

- **Local maximum**: a peak lower than the global best, but with no uphill neighbor — hill climbing halts here, satisfied but wrong.
- **Plateau**: a flat region giving no gradient to follow, so the search wanders aimlessly.
- **Ridge**: a sequence of local maxima awkward to traverse, because every single-axis move steps downward even though the ridge as a whole climbs.

```python
import random

def hill_climb(start, neighbors, score):
    cur = start
    while True:
        nbrs = neighbors(cur)
        best = max(nbrs, key=score, default=None)
        if best is None or score(best) <= score(cur):
            return cur                 # local optimum: no uphill move
        cur = best
```

**Simulated annealing** escapes local maxima by *sometimes moving downhill*. Borrowing from metallurgy, it picks a random neighbor and accepts an improvement always, but accepts a worsening move with probability $e^{-\Delta E / T}$, where $\Delta E$ is how much worse the move is and $T$ is a "temperature" that starts high and cools toward zero. Early on (hot), it wanders freely and hops out of bad basins; later (cold), it behaves like hill climbing and settles. With a slow enough cooling schedule it provably converges to the global optimum — though "slow enough" can be impractically slow.

**Local beam search** keeps $k$ states at once instead of one. At each step it generates all successors of all $k$ states and keeps the best $k$ overall. This is *not* the same as $k$ independent restarts: information flows between threads because the best successors may all come from one promising state, so the search concentrates effort where it is paying off. **Random-restart hill climbing** — just run hill climbing from many random starts and keep the best — is a dumb but remarkably effective baseline that often beats cleverer methods on hard problems.

The throughline: local search abandons completeness and any guarantee of finding *the* optimum, in exchange for tackling enormous (even continuous or infinite) state spaces with constant memory. When the state space is too big to ever hold a frontier, this is often the only option.

## 38.7 — Memory, time, and where search lives downstream

Choosing a search method is largely a negotiation between **time** and **memory**, and memory is usually the binding constraint. BFS and A\* give strong guarantees but can exhaust RAM long before they exhaust time, because they hoard the frontier. The iterative-deepening family (IDS, IDA\*) and local search exist mainly to buy back memory: IDS recomputes shallow nodes to avoid storing them; local search keeps essentially nothing. A useful rule of thumb is that if your frontier won't fit in memory, reach for an iterative-deepening or local method before a breadth-style one.

The same tension drives the heuristic question. A more accurate $h$ expands fewer nodes (less time and memory) but costs more to compute per node, and may be harder to prove admissible. The art is finding a heuristic that is informative, cheap, and provably optimistic — often via the relaxed-problem trick of §38.5.

Search is not an island; it is the substrate beneath much of symbolic AI:

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    S[State-space search] --> P[Planning: states are world configs, actions are operators]
    S --> G[Game playing: minimax/alpha-beta over move trees]
    S --> C[Constraint satisfaction: backtracking is DFS over assignments]
    S --> O[Optimization: local search over solution space]
```

**Planning** is search where states are descriptions of the world and actions are operators with preconditions and effects. **Adversarial game playing** is search over a tree of moves where an opponent picks the worst branch for you — minimax and alpha-beta pruning are search algorithms. **Constraint satisfaction** solving is essentially depth-first search over partial assignments with smart backtracking. Recognizing a new problem as "really just search" is one of the most transferable skills in classical AI — once formulated, the whole toolbox above applies unchanged.

## 38.8 — Key takeaways

- A search problem is five things: states, an initial state, actions/successors, a goal test, and a path cost. Formulation — abstracting the state down to what matters — is half the battle.
- All these algorithms share one loop over a **frontier**; they differ only in the *order* nodes are expanded. Graph search adds a visited set to avoid re-expanding states, trading memory for guaranteed termination.
- Uninformed search (BFS, DFS, UCS, IDS) uses no goal information. **Iterative deepening** is the practical default: BFS-like completeness and optimality at DFS-like $O(bd)$ memory. Use **uniform-cost** when step costs differ.
- **A\*** ranks nodes by $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ and is optimal when $h$ is **admissible** (never overestimates); **consistency** additionally means closed states never need reopening. A\* is optimally efficient for a given heuristic; build heuristics by solving relaxed problems, and take the max of several admissible ones.
- When only the final state matters, **local search** (hill climbing, simulated annealing, beam search, random restarts) handles vast spaces in constant memory, at the cost of completeness and optimality guarantees.
- The recurring tradeoff is time vs. memory, with memory usually the binding constraint — and search underlies planning, game playing, and constraint satisfaction.

## 38.9 — See also

- **Chapter 40 — Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing**: where minimax, alpha-beta, and backtracking search are developed in full.
- **Chapter 39 — Knowledge Representation & Reasoning**: how states and goals get represented symbolically for planners and provers.
- **Chapter 41 — Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics**: population-based optimization that generalizes local search (genetic algorithms, swarm methods).
- **Chapter 23 — Reinforcement Learning Foundations**: sequential decision-making when the successor function is unknown or stochastic, the probabilistic successor to state-space search.
- **Chapter 02 — Mathematics for Machine Learning**: the complexity, graph, and probability background behind these guarantees.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 37 · 🚨 Anomaly Detection](ch37.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [39 · 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning →](ch39.html)
